---
title: "Lecture 2: Expectations Hypothesis, Interest-Rate Risk, and Duration"
number-sections: true
---

<!-- AUTO-GENERATED by scripts/convert.py — do not edit. -->

The lecturer opened Week 2 with a short administrative segment (Group A
assignments, the reshuffle of Groups 16 and 18, the reminder that Group B
begins with Homework 3) and then spent roughly half an hour reviewing the
Lecture 1 material --- the spot curve, the YTM curve, forward rates, and the
bootstrap/OLS/Nelson--Siegel machinery --- before taking up the Expectations
Hypothesis in earnest. The lecture then runs through an empirical dismantling
of the EH (three facts, both unconditional and conditional), a topical case
study on the 2022--2024 inverted yield curve, and, after the break, a turn to
interest-rate risk: the observable volatility pattern in a long Treasury's
price series, followed by a first-pass formal treatment of duration and
convexity from the companion slide deck *L.2 Slides --- Duration and
Convexity*. Floating-rate notes (notebook 2.4) were flagged but not covered
and are deferred to Lecture 3.

# Opening --- Review of Lecture 1 {#L2-sec:review}

The lecturer used the first thirty minutes as a conceptual retread, and it is
worth keeping the main points on the page for later reference. There was no
new slide to reproduce; he was scrolling through the previously-covered
notebooks *1.1. The Spot Discount Curve* and *1.2. Forward Rates*
and answering live questions.

The first pass was on the *spot curve versus the YTM curve*. He
emphasized that what we did in Lecture 1 was *not* curve-fitting the
black YTM dots with a smooth red line. Black prices a bundle (a particular
bond, cashflow schedule and all), red prices a time (a single risk-free dollar
delivered on a particular future date). In a perfectly flat yield environment
the two coincide; in an upward-sloping environment the YTM is too high for
short cashflows and too low for long ones, so the bond's single YTM
*averages out* these compensating errors, leaving the spot curve
*above* the YTM curve at the long end. In the actual 2023 data the yield
curve is mildly downward-sloping, so the roles flip and the spot curve sits
*below* the YTM curve. The lecturer stressed that if the student cannot
reason this sign out without computing anything, "you didn't do enough on
week one."

:::{.callout-tip title="Filling the gap"}

**Filling the gap.** The lecturer argued the direction verbally but never
wrote the identity. For a coupon bond priced off a spot curve $r(t,T_i)$,
$$P \;=\; \sum_i \frac{c_i}{\bigl(1+r(t,T_i)/n\bigr)^{n(T_i-t)}}
         \;=\; \sum_i \frac{c_i}{\bigl(1+y/n\bigr)^{n(T_i-t)}},$$
where the second equality *defines* the YTM $y$. If the spot curve is
upward-sloping, the left equation uses small $r(t,T_i)$ on short cashflows and
large $r(t,T_i)$ on long ones; the right equation uses the same $y$ on every
one. For the sums to match, $y$ must be a compromise --- too large for the
short discount rates, too small for the long discount rates. A
cashflow-weighted average is the easiest way to see this, which is also the
Macaulay-duration decomposition that reappears in §[5](#L2-sec:duration){reference-type="ref" reference="L2-sec:duration"}.

:::

The second pass was on the *linear-system framing*. He reminded the class
that estimating the spot curve from a clean cashflow matrix $\mathbf{C}$ and a
price vector $\boldsymbol{p}$ is a linear system, which may be exactly
identified (bootstrap), over-identified (ordinary least squares), or
under-identified (stuck --- must impose structure). The problem in the full
quote file is that the matrix is wildly under-identified at maturities like
year 11 where there are no bills or notes expiring: the columns for year 11
and year 11.5 in $\mathbf{C}$ are identical, giving perfect
multicollinearity. Nelson--Siegel resolves this by imposing a smooth,
parametric curve rather than discount factors at every cashflow date, and the
lecturer said this is what a real bank or asset manager would use in
production.

The third pass was on *forward rates*. He re-stated the one thing a
student on a desert island must remember:
$$F(t,T_1,T_2) \;=\; \frac{Z(t,T_2)}{Z(t,T_1)}.$$
The forward discount factor is simply the ratio of the two spot discount
factors; forward rates are not a separate market object but a mechanical
transformation of the spot curve. In response to a student's question about
pricing derivatives "through the volatility of the spot curve day-to-day,"
the lecturer made a clean distinction: the forward curve at an instant is
*not a forecast*. It is the only no-arbitrage quote you can give for a
forward loan *at this instant*, given the spot curve at this instant. Ten
seconds from now the spot curve moves; the forward curve moves with it; that
is not volatility being mis-modeled, it is the consistency requirement
between the two sides of the same coin.

With the review complete ("you should be comfortable with this now"), the
lecturer pulled up notebook *1.3. The Expectations Hypothesis* and began
the day's substantive material.

# The Expectations Hypothesis {#L2-sec:EH}

## Motivation and Three Equivalent Statements {#L2-sec:EH-forms}

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 2--6.** *What Determines the Shape of the Term Structure?*\
Many theories, including...

- *Segmented markets.* Long- and short-term bonds appeal to
  different investors with differing supply and demand. This theory is
  at odds with the tight factor structure (high correlation) seen
  between bonds of different maturities, though it may explain some
  frictions.

- *Economic explanations.*

  - Liquidity preference theory.

  - Representative agent models.

  These are used more in macroeconomic theorizing, not in pricing or
  managing financial products.

**The baseline theory is the Expectations Hypothesis.** It is simplistic.
It is empirically wrong. But it serves as a useful baseline in organizing the
question.

:::

The lecturer opened the notebook with the framing that there are several
theories for why the yield curve takes the shape it does, and one --- the
Expectations Hypothesis (EH) --- dominates the conversation even though
everyone in finance knows it fails. The reason, he said, is pragmatic: the
full problem "how do I have a joint view on every rate at every maturity for
every future date?" is unmanageable, and imposing *no* structure means
you will write down models for each rate that violate no-arbitrage with each
other. The EH goes to the opposite extreme: all the interconnections collapse
to the statement that a single object --- the current spot curve, and
therefore the current forward curve --- encodes the market's expectations of
every future rate. "It is a pretty good starting point," he said. "If I
were going into an interview in five minutes and needed to talk about where
investors expect rates to go, the first thing I would look at is what
forwards have encoded about those expectations." The rest of the lecture
tests whether that shortcut is justified.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 7--10.** *The Expectations Hypothesis (EH).*\
The Expectations Hypothesis has three equivalent forms. Conceptually, they are:

1.  Long-term yields are the market's expectation of the average future
    short-term yields.

2.  Forward rates are the optimal forecasts of future (one-period)
    yields.

3.  Expected returns on holding bonds are equal across maturities.

**Mathematically.** The $n$-period yield is the average of expected
one-period yields:
$$y^{(n)}_t \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\,\E_t\!\left[
     y^{(1)}_t + y^{(1)}_{t+1} + \cdots + y^{(1)}_{t+n-1}
  \right].$$
Equivalently, the forward rate is the expected future spot:
$$f_t^{(n,n+1)} \;=\; \E_t\!\left[y^{(1)}_{t+n}\right].$$
Or, in terms of returns:
$$\E_t\!\left[r^{(n)}_{t+1}\right] \;=\; y^{(1)}_t \qquad \text{for every $n$.}$$

:::

A student offered statement 3 first ("rolling short-term bonds gives the same
expected return as buying and holding a long-term bond"), and the lecturer
used it as the entry point. The full set, he emphasized, is
*equivalent*: statement 2 says the orange forward curve you drew in
Lecture 1 is not just a mechanical no-arbitrage identity but also the market's
best guess for how the short-term spot curve will evolve over time; statement 1
expresses the same idea with log yields on both sides; statement 3 expresses
the same idea as a statement about expected excess returns.

:::{.callout-tip title="Filling the gap"}

**Filling the gap.** Statements 1, 2, 3 look like three different facts
but they are algebraic rearrangements of each other under continuous
compounding. From statement 1,
$$n\,y^{(n)}_t \;=\; \E_t\!\bigl[\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}y^{(1)}_{t+i}\bigr].$$
Differencing the same equation at maturity $n+1$ and at maturity $n$ gives
$$(n+1)y^{(n+1)}_t - n\,y^{(n)}_t \;=\; \E_t\!\bigl[y^{(1)}_{t+n}\bigr].$$
The left-hand side is the definition of the one-period forward rate
$f_t^{(n,n+1)}$, which recovers statement 2. Statement 3 follows from pricing
a zero-coupon bond at $t$ and at $t+1$ and equating expected log returns under
the risk-neutral condition. The lecturer skipped this derivation because it
is purely algebraic and the conceptual content is all in statement 2.

:::

The lecturer paused on the strength of statement 3. For a student with the
previous quarter's portfolio course in hand, this is the most damning form:
the EH asserts that there is *no risk premium* for holding long-term
bonds, which in a factor-model language means long-term bonds have zero
exposure to any priced factor. That would be astonishing. Students offered
candidate risks --- credit, liquidity, rate volatility, reinvestment risk,
inflation --- and the lecturer highlighted the inflation point as the most
compelling: locking in a five-year bond today exposes the holder to unexpected
inflation over five years, whereas rolling one-year bills resets the rate
annually and largely sidesteps inflation risk. A rational risk-averse investor
would demand a premium for the five-year bet, which the EH says is zero. So
the EH is bold.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cell 11.** *Testable Implications.*\

- On average, all yields are equal (from statement 1: long-term yields
  are averages of expected short-term yields, so over long samples they
  coincide).

- The current forward rate and the future short-term yield are equal on
  average (statement 2).

- Excess returns (long-bond return minus short-bond return) are zero on
  average (statement 3).

:::

## Empirical Analysis -- Three Facts Against the EH {#L2-sec:EH-empirics}

The lecturer grouped the empirical analysis into three "facts," each the
unconditional version of one of the EH's three statements.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 14--18.** *Fact #1. Long-term yields are higher than
short-term yields --- even averaged over long periods of time.* This means
long-term yields cannot simply be an average of short-term yields.

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c18_fact1_unc.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The table reports the unconditional mean yield at each maturity over the full
Fama--Bliss sample.

:::

The lecturer pointed at the table and said: if statement 1 held, all rows
would be (roughly) equal. They are not. The one-year yield averages about
$4.5\%$ while the five-year yield averages about $5.0\%$. Over many decades
the long-term yields are persistently higher than the short-term yields. A
student asked whether this could be a "lurking variable" --- some slow-moving
macro factor --- and the lecturer accepted the point: *something* beyond
the EH is driving the positive mean term spread, and the most natural
candidate is a risk premium for inflation, not unlike the equity-risk premium
the class encountered in portfolio theory last quarter.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 19--22.** *Fact #2. Forward rates are higher on average
than the future yield.* The forward overestimates the realized future spot.

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c22_fact2_unc.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The left column is the average forward rate $f_t^{(n,n+1)}$; the right column
is the average realized one-year yield at $t+n$. If statement 2 held they
would agree; they don't.

:::

The lecturer's analogy was the biased weatherman: it is winter, he forecasts
35 degrees; it is summer, he forecasts 90. He is "getting something right"
in the sense that the forecast tracks the season. But he is systematically
three degrees too high, and that is a failure of *optimality*, even if
his forecast is not useless. Forward rates in the data do the same thing ---
they overestimate the realized future spot by what looks like a constant term
premium at each maturity. The lecturer noted in passing that this is
consistent with forwards being the risk-*neutral* expectation of the
future spot, with the true physical expectation sitting below because of
investor risk aversion.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 23--26.** *Fact #3. Excess returns are not zero.*

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c25_fact3_unc.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The excess return (long bond return minus one-year bond return) averages
positive at every maturity. **Conclusion.** The statistics above are
evidence against the Expectations Hypothesis. Specifically: long-term yields
are higher than short-term yields on average; forward rates overestimate
future spot rates; excess returns are systematically positive.

:::

The lecturer summarized the unconditional case as "three ways of saying the
same thing: there is a term premium." A student's follow-up asked whether you
could *rescue* the EH by allowing a constant offset --- let the left and
right sides of each statement differ by a constant $\alpha$, possibly one
$\alpha$ per maturity. The answer: yes, you bail out the unconditional
version. But the rescue is only cosmetic --- statement 2 still fails
*dynamically*, because the deviation between forward and future spot is
not constant. It varies in predictable ways with the shape of the curve. That
is the content of the conditional (regression) tests in the next subsection.

## Dynamic Tests -- Conditional Regressions {#L2-sec:EH-dynamic}

The lecturer then ran conditional (regression-based) versions of the three
tests. His framing: "Yes, on average the forward overestimates the spot by
some constant term premium. Fine --- I'll absorb that into the regression
intercept $\alpha$. What I really want to test is whether the *slope*
$\beta$ equals one: does the forward move one-for-one with the future spot?"
A $\beta$ not equal to one refutes the EH in a way no term-premium rescue can
fix.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 27--40.** *Dynamic Fact #1.* Use a forecasting regression
to test statement 1. The relation
$$y^{(n)}_t \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\,\E_t\!\bigl[y^{(1)}_t + \cdots + y^{(1)}_{t+n-1}\bigr]$$
becomes (after replacing the expectation with an observed sample average and
subtracting the current short rate $y^{(1)}_t$ from both sides to improve
statistical power)
$$\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\tilde{y}^{(1)}_{t+i}
    \;=\; \alpha^{(n)} \;+\; \beta^{(n)}\,\tilde{y}^{(n)}_t
         \;+\; \varepsilon^{(n)}_{t+n},$$
where $\tilde{y}^{(\cdot)}_t \equiv y^{(\cdot)}_t - y^{(1)}_t$.
Under the EH, $\beta^{(n)} = 1$ at every $n$.

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c40_fact1_dyn.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The estimated betas are $0.32$, $0.31$, $0.30$, $0.30$ at maturities $n=2,3,4,5$
--- nowhere near one --- and the $R^2$ values fall from $60\%$ at $n=2$ to
$44\%$ at $n=5$.

:::

The lecturer highlighted the positive signs of the betas (the direction of
the prediction is correct, which is why he said the EH is "a pretty good
starting point") but stressed that $\beta \approx 0.3$ instead of $\beta = 1$
is a serious quantitative failure. If today's five-year yield rises a percent
relative to the one-year, the EH says to expect the next five years of
one-year yields to average a full percent higher --- the regression says
expect about a third of that.

:::{.callout-tip title="Filling the gap"}

**Filling the gap.** The lecturer mentioned twice that subtracting
$y^{(1)}_t$ from both sides "improves statistical power." The reason,
which he glossed, is that yields are extremely serially correlated --- the
year-ahead yield is well-predicted by today's yield. A regression of
*future level* on *current level* will give an $R^2$ near $90\%$
just because yesterday predicts today. That is not a forecast skill, it is
persistence. Subtracting today's one-year yield removes the persistence
component and forces the regression to predict only the *deviation* from
persistence, which is the content of the EH claim. This is the same
"predict tomorrow's temperature minus today's" trick from the portfolio
course.

:::

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 41--47.** *Dynamic Fact #2.* Test statement 2 directly by
regressing the realized future one-year yield on the corresponding forward
rate. Again use the excess-forward form
$$\tilde{f}_t^{(n,n+1)} \;=\; f_t^{(n,n+1)} - f_t^{(0,1)},$$
and regress
$$y^{(1)}_{t+n} - y^{(1)}_t \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \beta\,\tilde{f}_t^{(n,n+1)} \;+\; \varepsilon.$$
Under the EH, $\beta = 1$.

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c47_fact2_dyn.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The betas are again far below one and decay to roughly one-quarter at the
longer maturities, with $R^2$ values that offer little prospect of
overcoming transaction costs.

:::

The lecturer was emphatic that a high $R^2$ is not what the EH claims.
"The EH doesn't say the forward perfectly predicts the future spot. It says
the forward is the *best* predictor --- which in regression language means
the $\beta$ has to be exactly one." The data says no. He noted that the
failure is larger at longer maturities: the ten-year-ahead forecast is
essentially uninformative, while the two-year-ahead has some content.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 48--56.** *Dynamic Fact #3. Excess returns differ across
maturities in predictable ways.* Define the *forward spread*
$$\tilde{f}^{(n)}_t \;\equiv\; f_t^{(n,n+1)} - f_t^{(0,1)}.$$
The forecasting regression is
$$\tilde{r}^{(n)}_{t+12} \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \beta\,\tilde{f}^{(n)}_t \;+\; \varepsilon.$$
Under the EH, *no* regressor should predict excess returns, so
$\beta$ should be zero. In the data:

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c55_fact3_dyn.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The estimated betas are *positive and non-trivial*: when the forward
spread is high, future excess returns tend to be high, meaning the forward
curve contains information about future returns that the EH forbids.

:::

The lecturer tied this back to the homework case study. "I did not ask you to
code up these regressions. I asked you to build a naive trade the EH says
should not have worked --- long the maturity with the highest yield, short the
maturity with the lowest --- and see if it makes money over the long run. If
the EH is right, your trade has zero expected excess return. If it makes
money over seventy years, you have indirectly estimated a non-zero $\beta$ in
this third regression." The answer, which he previewed at the end of this
section and returned to in §[3](#L2-sec:invyc){reference-type="ref" reference="L2-sec:invyc"}: the carry trade has earned
about $1.5\%$ per year with a volatility of $4.8\%$, for a Sharpe ratio of
roughly $0.33$ over the 1952--2025 sample. Not spectacular, but not zero
either, and interesting as a diversifier.

## Appendix: Mathematics of the EH {#L2-sec:EH-appendix}

The lecturer scrolled through the appendix cells quickly. They set up the
notation used throughout the empirical analysis and the student should know
how to reconstruct the formulas, even if the lecturer skipped them in class.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 58--68.** *Zero-coupon (discount) bonds and log notation.*
Zero-coupon (discount) bonds with face value \$1 have prices like $0.99$,
$0.95$ rather than $99$, $95$; yields are in decimals $(0.015, 0.02)$ not
percentages $(1.5, 2)$. The $n$-maturity bond has price $P^{(n)}$ and y
$Y^{(n)}$ satisfying
$$P^{(n)} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1+Y^{(n)})^n}.$$
It is more convenient to use logs. Define
$$p^{(n)} \;\equiv\; \ln P^{(n)}, \qquad
  y^{(n)} \;\equiv\; \ln(1+Y^{(n)}).$$
Then $p^{(n)} = -n\,y^{(n)}$, equivalently $y^{(n)} = -\frac{1}{n}\,p^{(n)}$.

:::

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 71--73.** *Log prices and log yields, data check.*

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c71_logtails.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The tails of `lprices` and `lys`; note that log prices are
negative (they are discount rates times maturity, with a sign flip).

::: center
![image](figures/L2_1_c73_logseries.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

:::

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 74--80.** *Forward Rate Summary and Data Note.*
The log forward rate of zero-coupon bonds has the simple formula
$$f_t^{(n,n+1)} \;=\; p^{(n)}_t - p^{(n+1)}_t
                 \;=\; (n+1)\,y^{(n+1)}_t - n\,y^{(n)}_t.$$
The data underlying the EH tests are the Fama--Bliss Treasury discount bonds
at annual maturities out to $T=5$ years. Maturities beyond five years are
omitted because CRSP/CMT-style Treasury indices require substantial
cleaning to give reliable discount-bond prices beyond that horizon.

:::

The lecturer closed the Expectations Hypothesis section (to paraphrase): "So
the unconditional tests fail by a term premium, and the dynamic tests fail by
a slope coefficient. The conclusion is not that the EH is useless --- it is a
much better starting point than the CAPM, say --- but that it is
*wrong enough* that you would not set it as your firm's internal model.
Now let's talk about where this takes us in practice."

# The Inverted Yield Curve Case {#L2-sec:invyc}

Before the break, the lecturer pulled up a companion page titled *The
Inverted Yield Curve* (not among notebooks 1.3--2.4 but linked from the course
site) to run a case study on the Estrella--Sack--Bernanke debate and the
2022--2024 inversion. The page has no notebook cells to reproduce in a
keyblock, so what follows is prose. A classmate who missed class should open
the page `The Inverted Yield Curve` on the site before reading.

## The Empirical Regularity {#L2-sec:invyc-empirical}

The lecturer's starting chart was a time series of the 10-year Treasury yield
minus the 2-year Treasury yield from the 1970s to 2024, with NBER recession
dates shaded in gray. The striking pattern: every time the 10-year-minus-2-year
spread goes negative (the yield curve *inverts*), a recession follows
within roughly a year or two. Every time. Except possibly now --- the
2022--2024 inversion was the deepest and most sustained since the 1980s, and
as of the lecture (January 2026) the United States had still not entered a
recession.

He noted that the specific choice of maturities (10-minus-2, 10-minus-1,
10-minus-3-months, etc.) is not crucial --- empirical research at the New York
Fed uses several variants and they all broadly agree. The signal is whether
the curve is inverted at all, not which maturities are used.

## The Estrella--Sack--Bernanke Debate {#L2-sec:invyc-debate}

The Harvard case lays out three views on *why* the empirical regularity
exists, through the lens of the 2006--2007 inversion preceding the 2008
recession:

**Estrella and Sack (Fed/Goldman)** take the expectations-hypothesis
view. If forward rates are the market's expectation of future spot rates,
then an inverted yield curve --- where forwards are below current spot ---
means the market expects *rates to fall substantially* in the future.
Rates fall when the Fed cuts, and the Fed cuts aggressively during
recessions. So the yield curve inverts *because the market anticipates a
recession*, not the other way around. Sack's stronger version of this view:
the correlation is not causal in either direction, the inversion and the
recession both derive from the same underlying phenomenon --- a market
consensus that a slowdown is coming.

**Bernanke (Morgan Stanley, at the time)** takes the structural view.
The relationship that held in prior decades need not hold now because of
changes in how monetary policy operates and how the demand for long-term
Treasuries has evolved. So even if the curve inverts in 2006--2007, that does
not imply a recession is coming, because the old mechanism has broken. As we
know now, a massive recession did follow. The lecturer drew out the parallel
to today: "For the last three years, people have been making the same
argument. Some said the inversion guarantees a recession; others, that this
time is different. So far it is coming out on the side of 'no recession.' "

## Forward Paths vs. Realized Spot {#L2-sec:invyc-forwards}

To illustrate how visibly the forward-as-forecast story fails, the lecturer
showed a time-series chart of the realized 1-year spot rate (solid blue line
from 2014 to 2024) with *forward-rate paths* overlaid at several
historical instants (orange dashed fans). At the beginning of 2018, when the
1-year rate was around $2.5\%$, the forward curve predicted rates would
continue to float around $2.5\%$ for several years. What actually happened was
a drop to near-zero by 2020. The next fan, drawn in early 2022 when rates
were still near zero, predicted a gradual rise to about $1.5\%$ over four
years. What actually happened was a spike to $5\%$ within twelve months.

The lecturer was careful not to frame this as a refutation of the EH
("the EH would say these forwards are the best forecast *on average* ---
in any single period they can be arbitrarily wrong"). But the visualization
is a useful corrective to the intuition that "forward rates are the market's
forecast in a strong sense." They are the market's forecast in the sense
that no one can systematically do better after accounting for transaction
costs and risk premia, but they can and do miss drastic realized moves.

## The Carry Trade over Seventy Years {#L2-sec:invyc-carry}

The lecturer closed the case by showing what the homework's naive carry trade
(long the high-yield maturity, short the low-yield maturity, rebalanced
annually) would have earned from 1952 to 2025:
$$\text{average excess return} \approx 1.5\%, \quad
  \text{volatility} \approx 4.8\%, \quad
  \text{Sharpe ratio} \approx 0.33.$$

He asked the class how to evaluate a Sharpe of $0.33$. It is lousy as a
standalone bet but potentially interesting as a diversifier if it has low
correlation with the student's other holdings. His point: the EH says this
number should be zero. It is not zero. That is the quantitative content of
Fact #3, and the entire fixed-income term-premium literature is built on
exploiting and understanding it.

Fifteen-minute break; the lecturer took one question on China's recent
Treasury sales (liquidity-preference / segmented-markets explanation of curve
shape), agreed it was "an open question" whether supply/demand effects at
specific maturities partly drive the pattern, and broke.

# Interest-Rate Risk: A Single Bond Over Time {#L2-sec:irr}

After the break the lecturer turned to notebook *2.1. Interest Rate
Risk*. His framing: the previous notebooks looked at the *yield curve*,
which is a range of rates quoted on a single date. Now we flip it: a range of
dates for a single bond issue.

:::{.callout-important title="Key concept"}

**Cells 2--7.** *Prices of a bond.*\
We have discussed the yield curve, which is a range of rates quoted on a
single date. Now we consider a range of dates for a single bond issue.

::: center
![image](figures/L2_2_c07_dirty.png){width="90%" height="80%"}
:::

The dirty price of a 30-year Treasury from 1992 through 2023. Note that the
price varies substantially; there are periods where it is well above par and
periods where it is near par. This is the dirty price, which includes
accrued interest of the coupons. The quoted clean ask price would differ.

:::

The lecturer's question to the class: "Why does this price move a lot early
in its life and sit almost flat late in its life?" The answer has two parts,
one obvious and one subtle.

**The obvious part** is mechanical: the "staircase" pattern in the
late-life dirty price comes from the semiannual coupons. Between coupon
dates, accrued interest accumulates, so the dirty price rises smoothly; on
the coupon date, \$3 of value leaves the bond (the coupon is paid), and the
dirty price drops discretely. The clean price (excluding accrued interest)
removes this artifact and is the series analysts typically plot.

:::{.callout-tip title="Filling the gap"}

**Filling the gap.** The lecturer's "selling a used car with a
\$5,000 suitcase inside" analogy makes the point: clean price = dirty price
$-$ accrued interest. The formal identity is
$$P^{\text{dirty}}(t) \;=\; P^{\text{clean}}(t)
       \;+\; \frac{c}{2}\,\frac{t - t_{\text{last coupon}}}{t_{\text{next coupon}} - t_{\text{last coupon}}},$$
for a semiannual coupon $c$. Both are real prices; the dirty price is what
changes hands in a transaction, the clean price is the one that behaves like
a market signal because it strips the predictable coupon-accrual component.
The plot above is the dirty price; the stepped pattern is the suitcase
getting removed at each coupon date.

:::

**The subtle part** is that the clean price is quiet at the end of life
*even during periods of large rate moves*. Rates fell from roughly $3\%$
in 2019 to near zero in 2020, and the 30-year bond's clean price barely
budged, despite the bond still having four years of cashflows remaining.
Students tossed out candidate explanations. One, the correct one, was:
interest-rate sensitivity has collapsed because the remaining cashflow stream
is short. A zero-coupon bond's price sensitivity to rates is proportional to
its remaining time to maturity (we'll derive this in §[5](#L2-sec:duration){reference-type="ref" reference="L2-sec:duration"}),
so a bond with four years left responds to a 1% rate move by roughly $4\%$
of price, while the same bond in its thirtieth year responded by roughly
$30\%$.

But the lecturer added a second cause: *rates themselves barely moved
in 2021--2023 for the segment of time visible in the plot*. The volatility of
one-year yields over those years was essentially zero; with no input
variation there can be no output variation, regardless of the bond's
sensitivity. So the flat end-of-life clean-price segment is partly low
sensitivity (short remaining life) and partly low rate volatility (an
artifact of the 2021--2023 period).

The lecturer then closed the 2.1 notebook with: "Now, we could measure
sensitivity statistically --- do a regression of daily price changes on daily
rate changes, get a beta. But the beta would be mixing thirty years of
different sensitivities (30-year bond, 29-year bond, ..., 1-year bond),
which is not useful. We need a *mathematical* notion of sensitivity that
says something specific about each point in the bond's life." He clicked
the **Slides** link in the table of contents of the course site and
opened the PDF deck *L.2. Slides --- Duration and Convexity*, which
occupied the rest of the lecture.

# Duration and Convexity {#L2-sec:duration}

The remaining hour was taught off the `L.2. Slides --- Duration and Convexity.pdf` deck, which the lecturer had open in Preview (page 1 of 33
visible at the start). The deck is linked from page 2.2 of the course site
and downloadable from there. Unlike the other notebooks, the slide deck is
not a Jupyter page with cells, so this section is written as prose that
walks a reader through the same slide sequence the lecturer showed; a
classmate who missed class should open the deck alongside these notes.

## The Taylor Approximation {#L2-sec:taylor}

**Slide 8, L.2 Slides.** The setup:
$$\frac{\dd P}{P} \;\approx\; -\,D\,\dd r \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,C\,(\dd r)^2,$$
where $D$ is *duration* and $C$ is *convexity*. Bond prices are
nonlinear in rates, so this is a Taylor expansion around the current rate.

The lecturer emphasized three conventions embedded in that formula. First,
the expansion is in *percentage* price change $\dd P/P$, not dollar price
change, so that $D$ and $C$ are maturity-scaled numbers rather than dollar
sensitivities. Second, the quadratic term carries a factor of $1/2$, just
from Taylor (one could absorb it into $C$, but convention doesn't). Third,
and most annoyingly, there is a *negative sign* in front of the linear
term --- the linear sensitivity $\partial P/\partial r$ is always negative for a
vanilla bond (higher rate means lower price), and the convention is to
strip the sign out so that we can report $D$ as a positive number. The
lecturer's aside: "If I could redesign the basics of fixed income from
scratch, I would eliminate that convention. Duration is a negative number; we
report $-D$ because someone thought positive numbers looked friendlier."

He also pointed out that stopping at the quadratic is the convention in
fixed income, even though one *could* go to third order. For vanilla
Treasuries the cubic term is so small as to be invisible on the
approximation plots --- "you'd be the only one doing it, and there would be
nothing of interest in it." Options products with negative convexity can
behave differently, but those are later in the course.

:::{.callout-tip title="Filling the gap"}

**Filling the gap.** The full Taylor expansion of $P(r)$ around a base
rate $r_0$ is
$$P(r) \;\approx\; P(r_0) + P'(r_0)(r-r_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}P''(r_0)(r-r_0)^2
  + \tfrac{1}{6}P'''(r_0)(r-r_0)^3 + \cdots.$$
Dividing by $P(r_0)$ and defining $D \equiv -P'/P$ and $C \equiv P''/P$, the
stated approximation follows. For a 30-year bond at $r=4\%$, a 1% rate
shock is $\dd r = 0.01$; the cubic term scales as $(\dd r)^3 = 10^{-6}$,
negligible even for a 5% shock.

:::

## Duration of a Single Cashflow {#L2-sec:duration-single}

**Slide 11, L.2 Slides.** For a zero-coupon bond paying \$1 at time $T$
under continuous compounding, $P = e^{-rT}$ so
$$-\frac{1}{P}\frac{\dd P}{\dd r} \;=\; T.$$

The lecturer dwelt on this result for a moment: for a single fixed cashflow
under continuous compounding, *duration equals time to maturity*. This
is the magical coincidence that makes the concept work. It also, he warned,
leads to a common misunderstanding --- "some of you have taken the
*definition* of duration to be time to maturity. That is not the
definition. It is a mathematical consequence for a single fixed cashflow. For
a mortgage-backed security or a floating-rate bond, the duration is not time
to maturity; you have to go back to the sensitivity calculation."

## Duration as a Linear Operator {#L2-sec:duration-linear}

**Slide 13, L.2 Slides.** Because differentiation is a linear operator,
the duration of a portfolio of cashflows is the value-weighted sum of the
durations of its components:
$$D_{\text{portfolio}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i\,D_i,
  \qquad
  w_i \;=\; \frac{\text{PV}(\text{cashflow}_i)}{\text{PV}(\text{portfolio})}.$$

A coupon Treasury is a portfolio of zero-coupon cashflows (a $0.5$-year
coupon, a $1$-year coupon, ..., and the principal). Each has duration
equal to its time to maturity. So for a coupon bond,
$$D \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i\,T_i,$$
the *value-weighted* average of the cashflow times. The lecturer was
careful about the weights: they are not the nominal cashflow shares (\$3
coupons divided by the \$100 bond), they are the present-value-weighted
shares. The coupon twenty-eight years out is worth far less than \$3 today,
so it gets a small weight; the coupon next month is worth essentially \$3
and gets a near-full weight.

:::{.callout-tip title="Filling the gap"}

**Filling the gap.** To see that differentiation preserves the linear
structure: if $P = \sum_i c_i\,Z_i$ with $Z_i = e^{-rT_i}$, then
$$\frac{\dd P}{\dd r} \;=\; \sum_i c_i \cdot (-T_i) e^{-rT_i}
                 \;=\; -\sum_i T_i\,(c_i Z_i).$$
Dividing by $-P$,
$$D \;=\; \frac{1}{P}\sum_i T_i\,(c_i Z_i)
        \;=\; \sum_i T_i\,\frac{c_i Z_i}{P}
        \;=\; \sum_i w_i\,T_i,$$
with $w_i = c_i Z_i/P$ summing to one. This derivation is a few lines but
the lecturer only stated the result; the weight structure is the key thing
to see.

:::

## The Macaulay Closed-Form Formula {#L2-sec:duration-macaulay}

**Slide 15, L.2 Slides.** For a textbook bond where every cashflow sits
exactly $i/n$ years from today (compounding frequency $n$, maturity $N$
periods) and with coupon rate $c/n$ per period and y-to-maturity
$\tilde{y} = y/n$, the duration has a closed form:
$$D \;=\; \frac{1}{P}\,\frac{1}{n}\left[
            \frac{c/n}{\tilde{y}^2}\left(
              (1+\tilde{y}) - (1 + \tilde{y} + N\tilde{y}(c - y)/c)\,(1+\tilde{y})^{-N}
            \right) + \frac{N(1+\tilde{y})^{-N}}{n}
          \right].$$

The lecturer did not write the formula on the board (he pointed to it on the
slide) and cautioned that this is useful only for the idealized clean case.
For real-world messy data --- a first coupon that's fourteen days away and then
six-monthly after that --- the practical route is numerical: compute the
present-value weights of each cashflow using the spot curve, multiply by the
time to each cashflow, and sum. The messiness of real cashflow timing is a
reason to prefer the numerical route at a production desk.

A subtle point the lecturer paused on: the duration above is defined as the
derivative with respect to a *parallel shift in the spot curve*, but the
closed-form formula above is expressed in terms of the *y-to-maturity*
$y$. These are different objects --- the spot shifts a curve; the YTM is one
number summarizing the bond. The reason the formula works is that for a
single bond, the derivative of $P$ with respect to the YTM and the derivative
of $P$ with respect to a parallel spot-curve shift are related by a simple
scaling factor. That scaling factor shows up again below as the difference
between Macaulay duration and modified duration.

## Dollar Duration, Macaulay, and Modified Duration {#L2-sec:duration-names}

**Slides 17--19, L.2 Slides.** The lecturer walked through the
"alphabet soup" of duration definitions and emphasized that they are all
the same derivative, rescaled in different ways.

- **Macaulay duration** $D$ is
  $-\tfrac{1}{P}\tfrac{\partial P}{\partial r}$
  with $r$ a parallel shift in the spot curve. It is a percentage
  sensitivity. This is the default "duration" if no qualifier is
  given, especially in research.

- **Dollar duration** is $-\tfrac{\partial P}{\partial r}$, the
  *dollar* price change per unit rate change. It is the Macaulay
  duration multiplied by the bond price. Useful for reasoning about
  position-level P&L: a \$5M dollar duration on a \$1M position means
  a 1bp rate move produces \$500 of P&L.

- **Modified duration** is the analogue of Macaulay duration, but
  where the derivative is taken with respect to the bond's YTM (not a
  parallel spot-curve shift). It is what the closed-form formula above
  actually computes. It relates to Macaulay duration via
  $D^{\text{mod}} = D / (1 + y/n)$ for semiannual compounding.

The lecturer was wry about the proliferation of names. "Did we really need a
whole new term just to say 'same derivative, dollar units'? I suppose so."
He emphasized that for real-world portfolios, Macaulay duration is preferred
because there are fixed-income products (mortgage-backed securities, options
on bonds) where the YTM is not well-defined --- you cannot take a derivative
with respect to something that doesn't exist --- whereas a parallel spot
shift is always defined. Modified duration is ubiquitous in bond-desk jargon,
though, and will appear in the Week 2 case study (bond arbitrage).

## Convexity {#L2-sec:convexity}

**Slides 22--27, L.2 Slides.** The second derivative enters through the
convexity term:
$$C \;\equiv\; \frac{1}{P}\,\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial r^2}.$$

For a single zero-coupon cashflow at time $T$ (continuously compounded),
differentiating $P = e^{-rT}$ twice gives $P'' = T^2 e^{-rT}$, so
$$C \;=\; T^2.$$
By the same linearity argument as duration, a coupon bond's convexity is the
value-weighted average of the $T_i^2$:
$$C \;=\; \sum_i w_i\,T_i^2, \qquad w_i \;=\; \frac{c_i Z_i}{P}.$$

The lecturer brought up the *Convexity Approximation* plot (slide 27 in
the deck), which shows the price of an 18-year, 3% coupon bond under
parallel spot shifts from $-5\%$ to $+5\%$, overlaid with the
duration-only approximation (straight line, below the true price everywhere)
and the duration-plus-convexity approximation (very close to the true
curve). His observation: the duration line *always underestimates* the
true price, meaning convexity is *always positive* for a vanilla
Treasury bond. This is the well-known "convexity bonus" of bond positions
--- you gain more when rates fall than you lose when rates rise by the same
amount. Mortgage-backed securities and callable bonds can have negative
convexity, which is the subject of the second half of the course.

The lecturer also pointed out the size of the convexity correction in
practice. For the 18-year bond shown, a $5\%$ parallel shift produces a price
change that the duration line misses by roughly $2\%$ of bond value --- a
meaningful correction when accumulated over a large portfolio, but
near-invisible for the $1$--$10$ basis-point daily moves a risk manager cares
about. This is why practitioners almost never bother with a cubic term: on
daily horizons even the quadratic term is a minor correction.

## Why Parallel Shifts? PCA of the Yield Curve {#L2-sec:pca}

The lecturer closed the duration/convexity discussion with a justification for
why every formula in the deck assumes *parallel* shifts of the spot
curve --- an assumption that is obviously wrong in detail but used anyway.

He scrolled back to notebook *2.1. Interest Rate Risk* (cells 62--70)
where a PCA of the daily yield-curve changes is computed. The result: the
first principal component of daily yield changes explains roughly $97\%$ of
the total variation, and its loading is essentially flat across maturities
--- *all yields shift by roughly the same amount*. The second principal
component adds a slope factor and captures most of the remaining $2.5\%$; a
third curvature factor captures nearly all of what's left. So when a risk
manager is thinking about tonight's market move, a level shock is by far
the most likely thing to happen, and a parallel-shift sensitivity (duration)
captures $97\%$ of the risk.

:::{.callout-tip title="Filling the gap"}

**Filling the gap.** The PCA is performed on the matrix of daily yield
changes $\Delta \boldsymbol{y}_t$ (days $\times$ maturities). The first
eigenvector (the level factor) has the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$, and
$\lambda_1 / \sum_i \lambda_i \approx 0.97$. The lecturer mentioned "just
defined up to a sign" because if $\mathbf{v}$ is an eigenvector, so is
$-\mathbf{v}$ --- the sign is a cosmetic choice made when plotting.

:::

The second (slope) and third (curvature) factors justify the existence of
*key duration*, a generalization of duration where the shock is not
flat but concentrated around a specific maturity. The lecturer said these
are used but are a small correction to the level-factor story: "If you want
to stress-test a portfolio before a Fed announcement, maybe you do a slope
duration. But day-to-day, parallel-shift sensitivity is what drives 97% of
your P&L." Beyond-parallel-shift extensions live in the deck's appendix
and may be briefly revisited in Lecture 3.

# Closing Notes {#closing-notes .unnumbered}

The lecture covered the Expectations Hypothesis (three equivalent
statements; three unconditional facts against; three conditional regressions
against), the 2022--2024 inverted-yield-curve debate (Estrella vs. Sack
vs. Bernanke), interest-rate risk framed through a single bond's 30-year
price history, and a first pass at duration and convexity from the
companion slide deck. The deck material --- Taylor approximation, duration
of a cashflow, linearity and portfolio duration, the Macaulay/modified/dollar
duration zoo, convexity, and the PCA justification of parallel shifts ---
occupied roughly the last forty minutes.

The lecturer closed by previewing Week 2's case study (*Treasury
Arbitrage*, a Harvard case using 2008 financial-crisis data) and a paired
exercise where the student computes duration and convexity numerically on
the 2023 Treasury sample. Floating-rate notes, which are notebook 2.4 in
the class 2 set, were not covered; the lecturer explicitly said Lecture 3
would finish duration and convexity in the first forty-five minutes and
pick up FRNs and the subsequent material afterwards. Lecture 3 also
introduces STIR (short-term interest rate) futures, which are the Class 3
`web.yml` pages.
